perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date of receiving it, all Confidential Information will be maintained in confidence by the receiving party, will not be disclosed to any third party or to any persons empl oyed in its business other than those having a need to know for the purposes set fo rth above, and will be protected with the same degree of care as the recei ving party normally uses in the protection of its own confidential and proprietary information, but in no case with any less degree than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information will be maintained in confidence by the receiving party, will not be disclosed to any third party or to any persons employed in its business other than those having a need to know for the purposes set forth above, and will be protected with the same degree of care as the receiving party deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit 5-year term and the 'reasonable care' standard and replacing it with 'the same degree of care as the receiving party deems necessary' creates ambiguity and may render the clause unenforceable. Ontario law requires a reasonable standard of care in contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Contract Interpretation in Ontario
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Reasonableness
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the five-year term and the reasonable care standard, the modified text introduces uncertainty and potentially contradicts the implied duty of good faith and reasonableness in contractual performance under Ontario law. The phrase 'as the receiving party deems necessary' lacks objective criteria, making it difficult to enforce and potentially allowing the receiving party to act in bad faith.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'reasonable care' standard and a definite timeframe, replacing it with a subjective standard ('as the receiving party deems necessary'). This introduces ambiguity and contradicts the implied duty of good faith and reasonableness, because the new language lacks objective criteria. The perturbation explanation indicates Ontario law requires a reasonable standard of care in contractual obligations. Without any law snippets provided, the assessment relies on the perturbation explanation, law explanation and the direct comparison of the original and changed text, which sufficiently demonstrates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If either party elects not to pursue any further business und ertaking, each party will promptly return upon request all tangible informati on including any and all copies thereof relating to all Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party elects not to pursue any further business undertaking, each party will retain all tangible information including any and all copies thereof relating to all Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly return upon request' to 'retain all tangible information' directly contradicts the purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to protect the disclosing party's information. Ontario law recognizes the importance of returning confidential information at the end of an agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Confidentiality in Ontario
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that each party will *retain* all tangible information, the modified text violates the fundamental principle of confidentiality, which requires the return or destruction of confidential information at the end of the agreement or when it is no longer needed. This contradicts the implied duty to protect the disclosing party's proprietary information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the purpose of a confidentiality agreement. The original text states that information should be returned, while the changed text states that it should be retained. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both confirm that retaining the information violates the fundamental principle of confidentiality which requires the return or destruction of confidential information at the end of the agreement, resulting in a clear non-compliance.
